ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for grilled lean veal shoulder steak. Code A00LC (veal meat) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor; preparation facets 'grilled' and 'lean' are noted contextually.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for grilled lean veal shoulder steak. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular cooked-meat food codes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled lean veal shoulder steak is approximately 150 g, representing one medium steak portion with all visible fat removed before or after grilling.

gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lean veal shoulder steak contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (113 mg/100 g) with a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (potassium 245 mg/100 g); saturated fat approximately 2.1 g/100 g poses minimal arterial stiffness risk.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lean veal shoulder steak is suitable for individuals managing hypertension when prepared without salty marinades, high-sodium sauces, or added salt; the naturally low sodium and moderate potassium content support blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar result in no glycaemic impact; high protein content (27.3 g/100 g) supports satiety and lean muscle maintenance without raising blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lean veal shoulder steak is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a blood-glucose-neutral protein source; avoid sweet glazes, honey-based marinades, or starchy accompaniments that would introduce carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 77 mg/100 g and saturated fat at approximately 2.1 g/100 g are moderate; as a lean red meat, regular consumption should be monitored within overall dietary saturated fat limits.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may include lean grilled veal shoulder steak in moderation (up to 2–3 servings per week) as part of a heart-healthy diet, prioritising lean cuts, avoiding added saturated fats during cooking, and balancing intake with oily fish and plant proteins.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can amplify pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, even in lean cuts.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including lean veal to 1–2 servings per week, substituting with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as oily fish, and pairing with anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric or ginger when consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled veal shoulder steak (lean) is a pale red, tender cut of meat taken from the shoulder portion of a young calf, cooked on a grill until fully done with a lightly charred exterior. All separable fat is removed before or after cooking, yielding a very lean protein source. Per 100 g, it provides 159 kcal, 27.3 g protein, 5.5 g fat (of which approximately 2.1 g is saturated), and zero carbohydrates or sugar, making it a blood-glucose-neutral food with no glycaemic impact. Sodium is low at 113 mg and potassium is 245 mg, giving a favourable sodium-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is 77 mg per 100 g. The texture is firm yet tender, with a mild, slightly sweet flavour characteristic of young veal. Veal is widely used in Western European cuisines, particularly Italian, French, and German cooking traditions, and is considered a premium lean red meat. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension when prepared without added salt or sugar. For cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis, consumption should be moderated due to saturated fat and arachidonic acid content. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when sourced from appropriately slaughtered animals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lean veal steak?
Is veal shoulder steak high in protein?
Can I eat veal with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Input record carries HPB Foodo provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g values are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB database, an approved primary source. No estimation or analogy was required for core nutrient values.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as veal is a pure animal muscle protein with no carbohydrate content. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as meat contains no dietary fibre. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. Saturated_fat_g estimated at approximately 2.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for lean grilled veal shoulder (USDA SR Legacy Item 17199), as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat fractions. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item. Water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. Vitamin and mineral micronutrients beyond potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol set to -1: not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this specific entry. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null. This is a Western cuisine food; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under the multilingual rules. Food subgroup corrected from 'Beef' (input) to 'Veal' as this is a distinct species category (young calf, not adult bovine).
